perturbation.original_text: Such Confidential Information shall be identified in writing and marked ''Confidential '' by the disclosing party or, if first presented orally or visually, shall be reduced to writing, the writing marked ''Confidential'' by the disclosing party and delivered to the receiving party within twenty (20) days of the oral or visual disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Such Confidential Information should be identified in some format by the disclosing party. While marking the information as 'Confidential' is encouraged, it is not mandatory.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be identified in writing and marked' to 'should be identified in some format' and replacing 'shall' with 'is encouraged, it is not mandatory' weakens the obligation to properly identify Confidential Information. This introduces an in-text contradiction because other sections of the contract assume that information will be clearly marked, and the lack of mandatory marking creates confusion and potential disputes about what is truly confidential. If confidential information is not identified, the other sections of the document that reference it as confidential will become ambiguous and contradictory.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Exceptions to Confidentiality Obligations . The obligations of confidentiality and non- use shall not apply to Confidential Information which the receiving party can document: (a) at the time of disclosure was generally known to the public or, after such disclosure, became generally known to the public other than by a breach of this Agreement by the receiving party; (b) was already in the possession of the receiving party at the time of such disclosure without an obligation of confidentiality; (c) was later received on a non- confidential basis by the receiving party from a third party having t he right to impart such Confidential Information; or (d) is developed by an employee of the receiving party who did not have access to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates that Confidential Information 'shall be identified in writing and marked ''Confidential''.' The changed text weakens this requirement, stating that marking the information as 'Confidential' 'is encouraged, it is not mandatory.' This creates a contradiction with the 'Exceptions to Confidentiality Obligations' section. That section assumes that information is clearly marked to determine if an exception applies (e.g., whether information was already publicly known). If information is not clearly marked as confidential, it becomes difficult or impossible to apply the exceptions, resulting in a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees to treat Confidential Information disclosed to it by the other with the same degree of care as it does in protecting its own confidential and proprietary information, and the Confidential Information shall be disclosed within the recipient party only on a need- to-know basis.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees to treat Confidential Information disclosed to it by the other with reasonable care, and the Confidential Information should be disclosed within the recipient party as deemed necessary.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'same degree of care as it does in protecting its own' with 'reasonable care' lowers the standard of care required. Changing 'only on a need-to-know basis' to 'as deemed necessary' broadens the scope of permissible disclosure within the recipient party. This creates an in-text contradiction with the overall intention of the agreement, which is to strictly protect Confidential Information and its exclusive usage to 'the purpose'. If reasonable care is provided instead of the care given for their own confidential information, and its disclosure is as 'deemed necessary' then it creates a contradiction with the purpose and essence of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Survival . Termination of this Agreement for any reason shall not relieve either party of the obligations of confidentiality and non- use respecting Confidential Information disclosed to such party , which shall survive the termination of this Agreement for a period of ten (10) years, or i n the case of a trade secret, such Confidential Information shall survive until the Confidential Information no longer qualifies as a trade secret .
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the standard of care for Confidential Information from 'same degree of care as it does in protecting its own' to 'reasonable care' and broadens the permissible disclosure from 'only on a need-to-know basis' to 'as deemed necessary'. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that confidentiality and non-use obligations survive termination for a period of ten years or indefinitely for trade secrets. The lower standard of care and broader disclosure weakens the protection of Confidential Information, making the survival clause less effective and creating a direct conflict with the intention of the agreement to stringently protect said information. The change is from a high standard of protection to a weaker protection, therefore there is a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations of confidentiality and non- use shall not apply to Confidential Information which the receiving party can document: (a) at the time of disclosure was generally known to the public or, after such disclosure, became generally known to the public other than by a breach of this Agreement by the receiving party; (b) was already in the possession of the receiving party at the time of such disclosure without an obligation of confidentiality; (c) was later received on a non- confidential basis by the receiving party from a third party having t he right to impart such Confidential Information; or (d) is developed by an employee of the receiving party who did not have access to the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of confidentiality and non- use shall not apply to Confidential Information which: (a) is generally known to the public; (b) was already in the possession of the receiving party; (c) was later received by the receiving party from a third party; or (d) is developed by an employee of the receiving party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'can document' requirement significantly weakens the burden of proof for claiming an exception to confidentiality. The original text required the receiving party to provide evidence that the information fell under one of the exceptions. The removal of this requirement introduces an in-text contradiction with the intent of protecting the disclosing party, because it allows the receiving party to simply claim that an exception applies without providing supporting evidence. This creates an in-text contradiction because now is easier for the 'receiving party' to breach the contract and claim it falls under one of these exceptions.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Confidentiality Obligations . Each party shall maintain in confidence Confidential Information obtained from the other and use such Confidential Information only for internal purposes. Such Confidential Information should be identified in some format by the disclosing party. While marking the information as 'Confidential' is encouraged, it is not mandatory. Each party agrees to treat Confidential Information disclosed to it by the other with reasonable care, and the Confidential Information should be disclosed within the recipient party as deemed necessary.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the 'can document' requirement, weakening the burden of proof for claiming an exception to confidentiality. The original text required the receiving party to provide evidence that the information fell under one of the exceptions. Removing this requirement directly contradicts the 'Confidentiality Obligations' outlined in the contradicted text, which expects each party to treat confidential information with reasonable care. By making it easier for the receiving party to claim an exception without providing evidence, the change undermines the obligation to maintain confidentiality and creates a direct conflict with the intent of protecting the disclosing party, as per the Perturbation Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: All written, printed, electronically stored or other tangible documents, samples and materials submitted by one party to the other hereunder, and all copies thereof remaining in the receiving party’s possession, shall promptly be returned to the disclosing party and electronic media erased upon request or upon termination of this Agreement, except that a copy of each may be retained by the receiving party under appropriate security.
perturbation.changed_text: All documents, samples, and materials submitted by one party to the other, and all copies thereof remaining in the receiving party’s possession, should be returned to the disclosing party upon termination of this Agreement, unless otherwise agreed upon.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'shall promptly be returned to the disclosing party and electronic media erased upon request or upon termination of this Agreement, except that a copy of each may be retained by the receiving party under appropriate security' to 'should be returned to the disclosing party upon termination of this Agreement, unless otherwise agreed upon' weakens the requirement to return confidential information and introduces ambiguity. By replacing 'shall' with 'should' and adding 'unless otherwise agreed upon', it creates an option for the receiving party to retain the information without a clear process or limitations, contradicting the objective of securing confidential information. The omission of the term 'electronic media erased' also adds to the ambiguity and weakens the security around the Confidential Information. This omission creates an in-text contradiction because other sections assume the return and secure deletion of all confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Survival . Termination of this Agreement for any reason shall not relieve either party of the obligations of confidentiality and non- use respecting Confidential Information disclosed to such party , which shall survive the termination of this Agreement for a period of ten (10) years, or i n the case of a trade secret, such Confidential Information shall survive until the Confidential Information no longer qualifies as a trade secret .
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation to return confidential information and allows for its retention 'unless otherwise agreed upon'. This directly contradicts the 'Survival' clause, which states that obligations of confidentiality and non-use survive termination for a specified period. The original text mandated return 'except that a copy of each may be retained by the receiving party under appropriate security' whereas the change weakens this obligation by changing 'shall' to 'should' and adding 'unless otherwise agreed upon'. This creates a contradiction because the Survival clause assumes the information is not only kept confidential, but also returned.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may at any time upon written notice terminate this Agreement; otherwise participation by the parties in the review or evaluation shall end, and this Agreement shall terminate ten (10) years from the Effective D ate.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement with reasonable notice; otherwise, this Agreement shall continue indefinitely.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'at any time upon written notice' with 'with reasonable notice' introduces ambiguity regarding the timeframe of notice required for termination. Removing the 'ten (10) years from the Effective Date' clause and replacing it with 'continue indefinitely' drastically alters the agreement's term. This creates an in-text contradiction because the indefinite duration clashes with the clauses about survival of confidentiality obligations, which are intended to have a limited duration ('Termination of this Agreement for any reason shall not relieve either party of the obligations of confidentiality and non- use respecting Confidential Information disclosed to such party , which shall survive the termination of this Agreement for a period of ten (10) years'), it creates an in-text contradiction. Additionally, if confidential information is no longer a trade secret then this is no longer applicable, and the rest of the survival and term condition become contradictory.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Survival . Termination of this Agreement for any reason shall not relieve either party of the obligations of confidentiality and non- use respecting Confidential Information disclosed to such party , which shall survive the termination of this Agreement for a period of ten (10) years, or i n the case of a trade secret, such Confidential Information shall survive until the Confidential Information no longer qualifies as a trade secret .
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces a fixed term ('ten (10) years from the Effective D ate') with an indefinite term ('continue indefinitely'). This directly contradicts the 'Survival' clause, which limits confidentiality obligations to ten years or until the information is no longer a trade secret. The original text ensured a finite end to the agreement and related obligations, while the changed text introduces an unlimited duration, conflicting with the survival clause's time limit. Therefore, a strong in-text contradiction exists.
